title: Scroll Triggers, Liquid Glass, Kinetic Type, Shine Borders & Base-Select
description: Five bleeding-edge platform patterns: discrete scroll trigger/reverse, refraction-based liquid glass, letter-split kinetic type, a spinning shine border, and the fully customizable native select.

drops.title: Scroll Trigger & Reverse
drops.description: Cards play a transition once crossing a trigger line on the way down, then reverse-play scrolling back up — discrete, not scrubbed.
drops.concept: The new CSS timeline-trigger/animation-trigger properties let a normal time-based keyframe animation fire forward once when scroll crosses an activation range, and reverse once scrolling back out — distinct from continuous animation-timeline: view() scrubbing. Reimplemented here with an IntersectionObserver (rootMargin approximating the activation band) toggling an .in class, paired with CSS transitions so each card's own recipe (slide+fade, clip-path wipe, springy scale, blur rack-focus) plays forward on enter and symmetrically reverses on exit — with a debug toggle to disable reverse-on-scroll-up entirely (one-shot mode).
drops.source: Chrome for Developers — CSS Scroll-Triggered Animations (animation-trigger / timeline-trigger)

interaction.mouse: Scroll the page slowly; each card triggers as it crosses the line and reverses scrolling back up
interaction.click: Change the trigger-point select or reverse toggle in the debug panel
interaction.touch: Scroll normally on mobile; debug panel hidden on small screens

drops.title: Liquid Glass Panels
drops.description: An SVG filter chain makes panels visibly refract the backdrop near their edges with a faint color fringe — past flat blur-behind glassmorphism.
drops.concept: backdrop-filter: url(#id) references a single SVG <filter> chain instead of a plain blur(). An edge-weighted radial-gradient mask (generated via feImage) is combined with feTurbulence noise via feComposite to build a displacement texture that's strongest near the panel border and near-zero at the center; feDisplacementMap applies it to the live backdrop for a lens-like refraction. feGaussianBlur adds the frosted core, and a channel-split (three feOffset+feColorMatrix passes recombined with feBlend screen) offsets red and blue slightly in opposite directions for a subtle chromatic-aberration fringe. All three stages are exposed as sliders that live-patch the filter primitives' attributes.
drops.source: nikdelvin/liquid-glass — Pixel-perfect recreation of Apple's iOS 26 Liquid Glass effect using only CSS and SVG filters

interaction.mouse: Drag the strength/blur/chromatic-aberration sliders to feel each filter layer independently over the drifting color blobs
interaction.click: No click required — sliders drive everything
interaction.touch: Sliders are touch-draggable; debug panel hides on small screens

drops.title: Letter-Split Kinetic Type
drops.description: One split-and-stagger system, four selectable motion recipes — flip, drop, scatter, wave — driven by a per-letter CSS custom property.
drops.concept: JS splits any headline's text into per-character spans and stamps each with --i (its index) plus, for scatter mode, randomized --dx/--dy/--rot custom properties for chaotic convergence. Staggering is pure CSS with zero JS timers: animation-delay: calc(var(--i) * var(--ls-stagger)) on every span. Mode switching just toggles an ancestor class (mode-flip/drop/scatter/wave); each mode's @keyframes reads the same shared variables differently — flip/drop animate rotateX with opposite transform-origins, scatter animates translate+rotate from the randomized offsets, wave overshoots upward then settles. A second headline demonstrates the scatter recipe firing on scroll-into-view via IntersectionObserver, and prefers-reduced-motion short-circuits straight to the final state.

interaction.mouse: Click the Flip/Drop/Scatter/Wave tabs to switch the first headline's recipe; drag stagger/duration sliders
interaction.click: Replay button restarts both headlines' animations
interaction.touch: Tabs and replay button are tap-sized; second headline replays on scroll into view

drops.title: Spinning Shine Border
drops.description: A light beam continuously sweeps a card's edge via two layered backgrounds and one animated @property angle — no JS, no pseudo-elements.
drops.concept: Two backgrounds are layered with background-clip: an interior linear-gradient clipped to padding-box, and a conic-gradient clipped to border-box, with the element's real border set to transparent so the gradient shows through as the ring. The conic-gradient is angle-driven via a registered custom property (@property --bg-angle { syntax: '<angle>'; }), which makes it interpolate smoothly under animation instead of snapping; conic-gradient(from var(--bg-angle) in oklch longer hue, ...) takes the long way around the color wheel so one hue endpoint produces a full rainbow sweep. The animation runs paused by default and switches to running on :hover (or a permanent .always-on class), keeping idle CPU at zero.
drops.source: CodeTV (Jason Lengstorf) — Animated CSS gradient borders (no JavaScript, no hacks)

interaction.mouse: Hover any card or the button to spin up its shine border; it pauses again on mouse-out
interaction.click: Toggle Always spinning in the debug panel; drag speed/border-width sliders
interaction.touch: Always-spinning toggle makes the effect visible without hover on touch devices

drops.title: Customizable Select
drops.description: Real, accessible <select> elements themed with appearance: base-select — flags, swatches, and avatars inline, with automatic native fallback.
drops.concept: appearance: base-select on both the <select> element and its ::picker(select) pseudo-element switches the closed button and the dropdown panel into a fully-stylable minimal rendering mode while staying a genuine, keyboard-navigable, form-submittable native control — no hand-rolled div dropdown. <option> elements can now hold arbitrary HTML (flag emoji, color-swatch spans, avatar initials), and an optional <selectedcontent> placed inside a <button> child automatically mirrors whichever option is selected into the closed state, so open and closed views share one source of markup. ::picker-icon and ::checkmark expose the caret and the selected-indicator for styling. Because the opt-in is progressive, unsupported browsers silently render the plain native select with zero JS feature-detection needed — a live CSS.supports() check in the panel reports which path the current browser is taking.

interaction.mouse: Click any select to open its themed panel; hover options to see the highlight
interaction.click: Use the keyboard after focusing (Tab, arrows, Enter) — it's a real native control
interaction.touch: Tap to open on mobile; native touch scrolling and selection work unmodified
